title: Do I keep it private or report it?

intro.description: A young person disclosed something concerning and asked you not to tell anyone — now what? This walks you through whether you can keep it private or need to report, and what to do either way.
standingRules: Respecting privacy is everyone’s job; deciding what gets shared and with whom is a Facilitator call.
When unsure, treat it as reportable and loop in @Facilitators.

moderator.banner: Moderators: whether to report is a Facilitator decision. Use this tool to understand the path, and escalate to @Facilitators with a message link as soon as anything here points to reporting.

closeout.title: Always close out
steps: Complete the Incident Report — what happened (dates, times, message links), who was involved, what you did and who you looped in, what was reported and when, and what follow-up is owed and who owns it.
Log it in #flags-log so the Facilitator team has the picture — keep sensitive detail appropriate to the channel.
Debrief and check on whoever responded. This work carries real weight; don’t let the responder just move on alone.
If the concern involves a staff member, run the Reporting Hierarchy tool too.

q1.text: Do you have reasonable grounds to suspect that someone’s life is in imminent danger?

hint.label: What are ‘reasonable grounds’?
hint.body: What an average person, using honest judgement, would need in order to decide to report. You report suspicion — you don’t need to be certain, and investigating is not your job.

q2.text: Do you have reasonable grounds to suspect the youth is experiencing abuse, neglect, or exploitation?

q3.text: Would contacting the emergency contact / trusted adult on file put the youth at more risk?

hint.label: How do I judge that?
hint.body: Ask yourself: are they the source of harm? Does the youth live with the person causing harm? Could they retaliate, pressure the youth, or tip off the perpetrator?

q4.text: Are you aware of the youth engaging in behaviour that concerns you — substance use, self-injury, disordered eating, illegal behaviour, high-risk activities?

q5.text: Are you aware of any irrevocable or serious consequences from that behaviour — major injuries, STIs?

q6.text: Are you aware of a pattern of increasing risk — higher frequency, more substance use?

q7.text: Is the youth withdrawing from friends, family, school, or things they used to enjoy — or worried, distressed, or feeling helpless to control their own behaviour?

q8.text: Are you aware of a caregiver or authority figure who already knows — a therapist, for example?

q9.text: Does the youth want another adult to know?

q10.text: Does the youth trust their parents or guardians in this context?

end-911.headline: Call 911 or relevant emergency services now.
steps: Use the city/province and emergency contact on file to direct help if the youth can’t.
Once everyone is safe: if this involved abuse, neglect, grooming, or exploitation, come back and pick it up in the Critical Incident Tool — some of these carry a legal duty to report.

moderator.headline: Call 911, then escalate.
steps: If a life is in danger, anyone calls 911 — you don’t wait for a Facilitator.
Then ping @Facilitators immediately with a link to the conversation.
Stay with the youth in DM if you’re the one they trust.

end-consult.headline: Ask the youth for further clarification, and consult.
steps: Gently ask the youth to help you understand more — you don’t need to interrogate, just clarify.
Consult colleagues or relevant experts — ping @Facilitators. You make better decisions with a second person.
Then come back through this tool.

end-report-only.headline: Report to the child-protection authority — without contacting the emergency contact.
steps: Loop in the Manager of Youth Programs — or the COO if unavailable — right away. Don’t carry this alone.
Contacting the adult on file could put the youth at more risk, so the report goes straight to the authority.

Being supported doesn't move the duty off you: if you have reasonable grounds to suspect abuse, neglect, or harm, the decision to report is yours, and no one can take it off you, tell you not to, or make you wait for sign-off. Your supervisor helps you think it through, find the right line to call, get it documented and followed up, and check on you after — they don't decide whether you report, and they only make the report for you if you truly can't. Loop in and report, in parallel.

actions.label: What to expect from your supervisor →

moderator.headline: Escalate to @Facilitators now — reporting is a Facilitator action.
steps: Ping @Facilitators immediately with a link to the conversation.
Don’t promise the youth secrecy, and don’t investigate.
Stay with the youth in DM if you’re the one they trust.

end-contact-report.headline: Contact the emergency contact / trusted adult AND report to the appropriate authority.
steps: Loop in the Manager of Youth Programs — or the COO if unavailable — right away. Don’t carry this alone.

Being supported doesn't move the duty off you: if you have reasonable grounds to suspect abuse, neglect, or harm, the decision to report is yours, and no one can take it off you, tell you not to, or make you wait for sign-off. Your supervisor helps you think it through, find the right line to call, get it documented and followed up, and check on you after — they don't decide whether you report, and they only make the report for you if you truly can't. Loop in and report, in parallel.

actions.label: What to expect from your supervisor →

moderator.headline: Escalate to @Facilitators now — reporting is a Facilitator action.
steps: Ping @Facilitators immediately with a link to the conversation.
Don’t promise the youth secrecy, and don’t investigate.
Stay with the youth in DM if you’re the one they trust.

end-support.headline: Continue to support the youth confidentially.
steps: No new report is needed — either nothing crossed the reporting line, or an appropriate adult already has it in hand (your answer trail shows which).
Keep the relationship strong.
If anything changes, come back through this tool.
If the risk keeps escalating, bring it to the scorecard meeting — supporting a youth confidentially doesn’t mean carrying it alone.

end-assist-parents.headline: Assist the youth to figure out how to communicate with their parents or guardians within a set timeframe.
steps: Help them plan what to say, to whom, and by when.
Log it per the #flags-log guide.

end-community.headline: Refer the youth to a community resource.
steps: Help them find support that’s able, available, and acceptable to them.
Log it per the #flags-log guide.
